in their man caves. There are all these equipment. It's like the deck of the starship Enterprise. And I'm noticing I've got three Harley riders in my platinum group. Two who, one who actually drove from New Jersey all the way to South Dakota where they have their meeting. And another who put the bike on the semi-truck and disembarked like two blocks away and put mud on himself and drove in. There's the smarter of the two. Exactly. And then there's the female version that shocked me too, which is one of my platinum members, looks like a female version of an accountant, but who is a David Gilmore fanatic and plays electric guitar secretly at home. So I want more of those folks, but I seem to be attracting a lot of like desperate people who are looking who don't have work. We don't want them. No, and so my question is- That's for running a charity or a soup kitchen or something. We don't want them at the running a business. There's something a little bit wrong with my marketing. Maybe I'm too accessible. I'm too touchy-feely or something. I don't know what it is. How do I diagnose what's going on? It could be message. Yeah. It could be, in terms of access, see, it could be how easy it is to access you online and get to you, because there's no broader net now than that. And so, see, I look to source is where I would look. So the ones you describe all have their Walter Mitty fantasy life and clearly have more than one in many cases and want more. So there's two places I would know to go hunt for them. One is the magazines of the people living the fantasy lives. So motorcycle riders have magazines. Skydivers have magazines, et cetera. The other would be the straight-laced professions that they are coming out of. So accountants have magazines. And everything in the accounting magazine is just as dull and boring and straight-laced as they are. So you stand out dramatically, stepping in there with, have you ever thought of, and I would be dominoing my champions. Say, so now I got those two Harley people, I'd be dying to get into that market, because I got them. So I got them to show off. That alone would drive me into testing that market. Everything I've just said takes you offline, which means it raises your cost of lead and it raises your cost of sale. But their value, see, is so much better than the other that it's probably an economic win. But see, the very good thing is, is that you've really identified a powerful and unique valuable psychology about this customer that you want. And so now you can tailor your whole message to this. And the deeper meanings that they're getting out of that. What are they getting out of that? Recapture of youth, adventure, right? So what are we about? We're for people with a sense of adventure. We're for people who still think and act and behave young regardless of their chronological age. We're for, see, what are we about? Who are we for? Celebrity, I mean, is the person who obviously rides Harley kind of wants to get attention? Well, yeah, he wants to be. So the aspect of being a voice over artist is attractive? Do I need to show myself having more adventures in stuff or is it enough to be the voice of AT&T? Well, I mean, look. You do go to Brazil. I think the more you exemplify the values and aspirations that attract them, the better off you are. And you already got stuff. You just don't use it. You're in Brazil, whatever you're doing over there, three times a year with the metaphysical, whatever you're doing, and march around to campfire. And you know, so I mean, you were on Howard Stern. That's an adventure. So this goes all the way back to our self-aggrandizement conversation. See, you don't do enough of that. Right. Right? You don't do, and you got it to work with. You have the raw material. You're just not using it. Okay, your point real quick, and then I have a few things I have to say, and then we gotta close. Thanks. I was on vacation last week. One of the books I read was Charlotton. And looking at the seven steps, as crazy as that guy was, immoral and everything else. He hits just about every one of them. I just, you might want to comment, and if anybody here hasn't read that. Well, if you haven't read Charlotton, you should read. First of all, it's a fabulous read, and it's a novelized account of a real person, Dr. John Brinkley, who's really an alternative health practitioner. Right. One way to put it out. From many, many moons ago. I am working on a book with a member of ours, whose wife is a direct descendant of Brinkley's. And we're working on a book and a program about Brinkley marketing. And they've got a mountain of archival material. We got radio broadcasts, we got brochures. We've got all kinds of stuff. It's really cool. Brinkley's claim to fame was he got people to come to, Ben to come to his clinic to have his goat, right? Goat. Goat testicles grafted on to improve their potency. This is a real surgery, by the way. It's the forerunner to enzite. But the book's fabulous. You should read the book. Oh, and he did the border blasters thing. The border blasters. He was the first one, really, the first one to use radio. I mean, it's way ahead of his time.
